pages.text: Properties of self-compacting concrete prepared with coarse recycled concrete aggregate Zoran Jure Grdic *, Gordana A. Toplicic-Curcic, Iva M. Despotovic, Nenad S. Ristic Faculty of Civil Engineering and Architecture of Nis, Aleksandra Medvedeva 14 St., 18000 Nis, Serbia article info Article history: Received 25 August 2009 Received in revised form 11 December 2009 Accepted 16 December 2009 Available online 22 January 2010 Keywords: Self-compacting concrete Recycled aggregate Compressive strength Tensile strength Water-proofness abstract Self-compacting concrete has signiﬁcant environmental advantages in comparison to the vibrated con- crete: absence of noise and vibrations during installing provides a healthier working environment. In the paper the potential for usage of coarse recycled aggregate obtained from crushed concrete for making of self-compacting concrete was researched, additionally emphasizing its ecological value. On the other hand the issue of the waste disposal sites created by the demolition of old structures is solved. In the experiment, three types of concrete mixtures were made, where the percentage of substitution of coarse aggregate by the recycled aggregated was 0%, 50% and 100%. In the process of mixing, equal consistence of all concrete mixtures was achieved. The obtained results indicate that the properties of these concretes have only a slight difference, and that the recycled coarse aggregate can successfully be used for making of self-compacting concrete. /C2112009 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Usage of self-compacting concrete have become very widely present and varied in recent years. Self-compacting concrete is such that after casting into the formwork, does not require vibrat- ing. Compaction of this concrete is accomplished in all the parts of the form, including the hardly accessible parts, with no additional external force, except the gravity, that is, as a result of the concrete weight. The ﬁlling ability and stability of self-compacting concrete in the fresh state can be deﬁned by four key characteristics: ﬂow- ability, viscosity (assessed by rate of ﬂow), passing ability and seg- regation resistance. The concrete mixture will be classiﬁed as a self-compacting one, if all the mentioned characteristics are pres- ent [1–3]. Such properties are achieved by addition of chemical additives to the concrete, such as superplastiﬁcators, most frequently com- bined with the new type of additives for modiﬁcation of viscosity [4], and/or through application of a certain amount of ﬁne mineral additive-powder. Properties of self-compacting concrete depend on type and amount of additive which is used, and there are numerous research papers which consider that subject [5–7].I n Ref. [7], authors analyze an inﬂuence of ﬂy ash, silica fume, lime- stone and their combination on self-compacting concrete proper- ties, and conclude that the best properties are achieved with silica fume, but it is the most expensive additive. Limestone itself or in combination with ﬂy ash, gives also very good results. Very fast increase in concrete strenght is explained in [8,9] as a result of use remarkable amounts of limestone in concrete mixes. The sustainable development concept, which apart from the sociological and economic aspects comprises energy saving, envi- ronment protection, and conservation of non-renewable natural resources, is a strategic orientation of numerous industries and a particular contribution

strength due to the use of recycled aggregate is more signiﬁcant in a weak concrete than in stronger one. The use of coarse aggregate made from recycled concrete with strength equal to 50 MPa will result in concrete compressive and tensile strengths comparable with that achieved when using natural coarse aggregate. Recycled concrete mixes require more water than conventional concrete to maintain the same slump without the use of admixtures. There are many different test methods for testing main charac- teristics of fresh self-compacting concrete, which are developed specially for this type of concrete. They are shown in [13–16]. Recommendations about mix design, which are important for experiment, are shown in Rilem reports, [17,18]. Regarding that it is a relatively new material, making of self- compacting concrete with recycled aggregate has still been inade- quately researched ﬁeld. The obtained results can be very signiﬁ- cant, both from the aspects of science and practical use. 2. Details of the experiment 2.1. Materials used in the experiment All the samples in the experiment were produced from CEM II/B-M (S-Q) 42.5N cement, whose properties have been presented in Table 1 : In the experiment rock ﬂour was obtained by grinding limestone of speciﬁc gravity 2.692 g/cm 3 and standard deviation of voids content according to Rigden was 0.23%. For making of concrete mixture the fractions of 0/4, 4/8 and 8/16 mm of the riv- er aggregate were used, and the fractions 4/8 and 8/16 of recycled aggregate. The recycled aggregate is got by demolition of 40 years old over bridge on road E10 Novi Sad – Subotica. Over bridge was made with vibrated concrete C 37/45 and natural aggregate from river Danube. The data about the particle size distribution of the aggregates have been pre- sented in Table 2 . A piece of data which is important for design of concrete mixture is the quantity of water absorbed by the recycled aggregate, as it is always higher in comparison to the same fraction of the river aggregate, and it is the consequence of existing resid- ual cement paste on grains of the recycled aggregate. For the used recycled aggre- gate, water absorption was: for the fraction 4/8 mm–5.88%, and for the fraction 8/ 16 mm–5.08%. In the experiment the additive Superﬂuid 21 M1M, of the plastiﬁcator type in agreement with EN934-2, which in chemical terms is a modiﬁed policarboxylate. Dose of 0.7% in respect to the mass of powder components (cement and rock ﬂour) was determined on the basis of the tests carried out prior to making the main con- crete mixtures. 2.2. Mixture design For the purpose of the experiment, three types of concrete were made: control concrete – made only with the river aggregate, test concrete P50 with 50% of recy- cled coarse aggregate which entirely substituted the fraction 8/16 mm and test con- crete P100, with 100% of recycled coarse aggregate where all of the coarse aggregate was substituted by the recycled aggregate. The quantity of components required for making of 1 m 3 of concrete was constant, with the exception of small variations in the quantity of water for the purpose of achieving the equal consistency and due to the slightly higher water absorption by the recycled aggregate. Composition of de- signed mixtures has been shown in Table 3 . For the already mentioned reasons, in the case of concrete mixture with 50% of coarse recycled aggregate (P50) the quantity of water amounted to 178 l, that is 186 l in the case of con

the end of the horizontal part and of the remaining concrete in the vertical part are measured ( H2/H1). That is an indicator of the capacity of concrete to pass through the rebars, and it should be as close to one as possible (the lowest permissible value is 0.8) [1]. Sieve stability test: the test examines resistance of self-compacting concrete by measuring the mass of fresh SCC which was passed through the sieve with 5 mm square apertures. The sieve dimensions are: diameter of 300 or 315 mm and height of 40 or 75 mm (ISO 3310-2). 4.8 ± 2 kg of concrete are poured on the sieve from the height of 50 ± 5 cm. After 2 min, the sieve is carefully, without shaking, removed from the vessel on which it was mounted and the quantity of mortar which passed through is measured. The percentage of passage of concrete through the sieve is a measure of segregation [1]. The hardened concrete was tested for compressive and tensile strength in the same manner which is also common for the vibrated concrete, so these procedures will not be explained here in detail. Water absorption testing was conducted on the samples of 12 /C212 /C236 cm dimensions, by the gradual immersion method. According to this method, the sam- ples are placed in an appropriate vessel and water is poured ﬁrstly to 1/4 of the height of the sample and after 1 h water is added to 1/2 of the height of the sample, and it is left so for 1 h. Then water is added to 3/4 of the sample height and the sam- ples are left for 20 h. Eventually, the samples are fully immersed in water where they are left for additional 2 h. The mass of water saturated samples ( m v) was for the ﬁrst time measured 24 h after the beginning of the test, and then at 24 h until the difference between two measurements is less than 0.01 g (until constant mass is reached). All the samples reached the constant mass after three days. Absorption is calculated by the formula H m =( mdw /C0md)/md /C2100 where md is the mass of the sample dehydrated to the constant mass in grams (accuracy 0.01 g), and mdw is the mass of the sample saturated by water until the constant mass. Water-proofness testing was conducted on the samples of 200 /C2200 /C2 150 mm, of the concrete age of 28 days. The samples were exposed to the action of water under pressure of 1 bar for 24 h, under 3 bars of pressure for the next 48 h, and under 7 bars of pressure during the last 24 h of testing. After this, the sam- ples are broken and the depth of water penetration is measured. 3. Test results and discussion 3.1. Results obtained by fresh concrete testing Results obtained by fresh concrete testing are displayed in Table 4: discrepancy in density between the control concrete, test con- crete with 50%, test concrete with 100% and of coarse recycled aggregate is 25 kg/m 3 and 35 kg/m 3, that is, 1% and 1.5%. Slump-ﬂow test examined ﬁrst of three key properties of SCC: ﬂowability. Distribution, in all three cases amounted to 73 cm which ranks all the designed mixtures in the SF2 class which is the most common class in civil engineering usage and practice. T500 is the time for which the concrete reaches the diameter of 500 mm and it is measured during execution of slump-ﬂow test. It represents the test of mixture viscosity, and for the SF2 class, an interval of 3.5–6.0 s is recommended. All the mixtures matched this requirement. Time longer than 2 s ranks them into the VS2 vis- cosity class. No segregation or water separation was observed. Fig. 2. L-box test –

L-box test, examined the second key property – passage of self- compacting concrete between the rebars without congesting. All the mixtures meet the criterion that the ration of heights of con- crete at the ends of L-box is no less then 0.8, and as the test was conducted with three rebars (which is also the requirement for densely reinforced structures) they were ranked to the PA2 class. All concrete mixtures were almost horizontal after ﬂowing out through the L-box opening, without blocking between the rebars (Fig. 3 ). Resistance to segregation as the third characteristic of fresh SCC was tested on sieve. The results demonstrate that all mixtures were resistant to segregation, the control mixture belonging to SR2 class SR2 (<15%), and P50 and P100 to SR3 class (<10%). 3.2. Results of hardened concrete testing Tests of compressive strength, after 2, 7 and 28 days yielded the results displayed in Fig. 4 : the density of the control concrete is higher for 51 kg/m 3, that is 2.12% in respect to the test concrete with 50% of recycled coarse aggregate and for 82 kg/m 3, i.e. 3.4% in respect to the test concrete with 100% of recycled coarse aggregate. Difference in strength for the same age cannot be characterized as signiﬁcant. The control concrete has a higher strength for 1.92 N/mm2, i.e. 3.88% in comparison to P50, and for 4.23 N/mm 2, i.e. 8.55% in comparison to P100 at 28 days of age. All three mixtures quickly gain early strength, and after 7 days they achieve more than 90% of the strength they have at the age of 28 days, which is the result of implementation of limestone ﬁller. The explanation of the certain reduction of strength in those concretes with recycled aggregate lie in this aggregate’s concrete microstructure. Namely, the main problem of application of recy- cled aggregate is its changeable and irregular and inconsistent quality, especially in the cases of the aggregate obtained by demo- lition of structural elements of a building. As opposed to natural aggregate, recycled aggregate has two components: natural aggre- gate and cement paste which is bound to it and which reduces its quality to a certain extent. The old cement paste is the cause of lower density, higher absorption capacity, lower abrasion resis- tance and high content of sulphates in the composition of the recy- cled aggregate in comparison to natural. The data from the available literature indicate that the aggregate fractions of 4/ 8 mm contain 33–55% of old cement paste, and the fraction of 8– 16 mm, contain less, as expected 23–44%. Content of cement paste is highest in small size fractions, thus their application is limited. In the course of setting and hardening, the new cement paste ﬁrst reacts with the old paste residue on the grains of recycled aggregate, which requires a part of the water that is a part of the concrete mixture composition, and reduces compressive strength. It is known that the cause of concrete failure is always in its weakest point. In the concretes made on the basis of natural aggre- gate, the failure occurs through the hardened cement paste, whereas in the samples with 50% and 100% of coarse recycled aggregate the failure path passed through the aggregate grains, since they are the weakest component of the composite (Fig. 5 left and Fig. 5 right). Results of tensile strength testing by bending are displayed in Fig. 6 . Results of the tensile strength testing by bending indicate that hardness of control concrete after 28 days is hi

mm2 or 2.49% in respect to the concrete with 50% of coarse recy- cled aggregated and for 1.01 N/mm 2 or 13.95% in comparison to the concrete with 100% of coarse recycled aggregate. This reduc- tion of strength was caused, as well as in the compressive strength case, by the changes in the concrete microstructure. The results of water absorbtion testing are displayed in Fig. 7 . The test results demonstrate that the highest water absorption was noted at the test of P100, 0.92%, which is for 0.15% higher than the test of P50 and for 0.37% higher than the control sample. The highest water absorption of P100 concrete is a logical consequence of implementation of recycled aggregate which has higher water absorption in respect to the river aggregate (5.88% for II fraction of recycled aggregate in comparison to the 0.94% of the II fraction of river aggregate). On the samples P50 and P100 no water penetration was re- corded, whereas the control sample had a penetration of 10 mm, so it can be concluded that all the samples are water-proof which is in full accordance with the structure of self-compacting concrete. 4. Conclusions /C15Extensive excavation of natural aggregate seriously endan- gered river econ-systems, so it has been prohibited in some areas. Apart from this, the increasing distance of natural sources from construction sites affects the cost of construction material. On the other hand, On the other hand, large quanti- ties of old concrete often occur in urban environments, result- ing from construction or demolition of old buildings, so removal and disposal presents an environmental problem. Well developed, environmentally aware countries pay due attention to material recycling and the charge high ﬁnes for waste matter disposal (as waste disposal sites occupy useful land). Crushing of concrete waste produces recycled aggre- gated which can be ‘‘returned” to production. /C15In the process of design of concrete mixtures, it is very useful to know the origin of recycled aggregate, because the higher quality of the original concrete, the higher quality of the newly made concrete. It is desirable that the aggregate be made from the same grade of concrete so that to have the most consistent quality. /C15The key property of a recycled aggregate is higher water absorption in comparison to the natural which is a result of residual cement powder. In order to apply the existing formula for concrete and achieve the same results as with the natural aggregate, it is necessary to increase the quantity of water. The required quantity of water can be compensated either by mea- suring of water absorption of recycled aggregate in ﬁrst 30 min or by its saturation via a prior immersion, or experi- mentally, until the same concrete consistency is achieved, which was the case in this paper. /C15Increase of the percentage of recycled aggregate reduces the density of concrete, which is an expected consequence of its increased porousness. Replace of 50% of coarse aggregate (whole fraction 8/16) decreased the density for 2.12%, and replace of 100% of coarse aggregate (fractions 4/8 and 8/16) decreased the density for 3.40%. /C15The quality of used recycled aggregate has a direct effect on the compressive hardness. If the quality aggregate is used, of a consistent composition, the differences in compressive strength between the control and test concretes with as much as 100% of coarse recycled aggregate can be small, as is the case in this paper.
